meta.status: POPULATED — research completed. DO NOT OVERWRITE — populated by research team.
sources: Charity Commission for Northern Ireland — charitycommissionni.org.uk
Clifton House Trust published charity model

legalContext.keyDifference: NI charities register with CCNI (not Charity Commission for England & Wales). Charitable Company Limited by Guarantee also incorporated at Companies House (UK-wide). Both registrations required.
legalContext.publicBenefitRequirement: All CCNI-registered charities must demonstrate public benefit under recognised purposes. For Fernhill: heritage preservation, community benefit, and education — all directly applicable.

models.name: Charitable Company Limited by Guarantee
keyFeatures: Full charity status — eligible for all heritage and community grant programmes
Gift Aid: 25p per £1 donated by UK taxpayers
Business rates: 80% mandatory relief (discretionary further 20% from BCC)
Trustees have fiduciary duties under charity law
Annual reporting to CCNI and Companies House
Can have wholly-owned trading subsidiary for commercial activities
taxAdvantages: Corporation tax exemption on charitable income
models.niCaseStudy: Clifton House Trust, Belfast — CCNI-registered charitable company managing Grade A listed Clifton House (1774). NLHF-funded restoration. Direct model for Fernhill.
models.suitabilityForFernhill: HIGH — required for NLHF, AHF, and HEF NI eligibility

models.name: Community Interest Company (CIC)
keyFeatures: Asset lock
Community interest test required
Can pay capped dividends
NOT a charity
models.criticalGap: NOT eligible for NLHF, AHF, or HEF NI capital grants. Cannot claim Gift Aid.
models.suitabilityForFernhill: NOT RECOMMENDED as primary vehicle — disqualifies Fernhill from key funders

models.name: Community Benefit Society (Bencom)
keyFeatures: Democratic governance — one member one vote
Can raise community shares
Asset lock
NOT a charity unless separately registered
models.suitabilityForFernhill: NOT RECOMMENDED as primary vehicle. Community share offer (£50k–£150k) can sit alongside charity structure as fundraising mechanism.

models.name: Hybrid: Charitable Company Parent + Private Ltd Trading Subsidiary
models.structure: Charity holds building and receives all grants/donations. 100%-owned Private Ltd runs all commercial operations (weddings, wakes, tea room, cinema, room hire). At year end, subsidiary donates profits to charity via deed of covenant — eliminating corporation tax on trading income.
keyFeatures: Charity holds building — maximum grant eligibility maintained
Trading subsidiary runs commercial operations — full commercial flexibility
Deed of covenant: subsidiary profits donated to charity annually — corporation tax neutral
Director overlap between charity board and trading subsidiary is normal
Clean separation protects charitable status from commercial risk
Standard model for UK heritage venues: Clifton House Trust, Wilton's Music Hall Trust, hundreds of others
pros: Maximum grant eligibility via charity vehicle
Gift Aid on all eligible donations
Commercial flexibility via subsidiary — no restrictions on wedding and hospitality operations
HMRC-efficient — no corporation tax on trading profits donated to charity
Standard model trusted by NLHF, AHF, HED NI
Separation of charitable and commercial risk
cons: Slightly more complex to set up

recommendation.primaryEntity: Fernhill Heritage Trust — Charitable Company Limited by Guarantee registered with CCNI and Companies House
recommendation.tradingSubsidiary: Fernhill House (Trading) Limited — 100% owned by the charity; runs all commercial operations
charitablePurposes: The preservation and restoration of Fernhill House for the benefit of the community
The advancement of heritage and education through access to a significant listed building
The promotion of community benefit through cross-community events and remembrance services
rationale: NLHF will only fund charitable or public bodies for building restoration — non-negotiable
HEF NI requires or strongly prefers charitable status
Gift Aid adds 25% to all eligible donations — significant over a multi-year campaign
Trading subsidiary protects charitable status while enabling full commercial operation

foundingTrusteeProfile.background: Legal or civic standing; heritage sympathy; Belfast credibility
Architect, conservation officer, or heritage sector professional in NI
South/west Belfast community representative; lived experience of the area
Events, hospitality, or commercial property experience
Cross-community credibility — trusted by both traditions

registrationTimeline.action: Identify founding trustees; agree structure; engage solicitor
Draft Memorandum and Articles of Association; incorporate at Companies House (£50)
Submit charitable registration to CCNI; apply to HMRC for Gift Aid
Open charity bank account (CAF Bank, Unity Trust, or Triodos)
CCNI registration received (estimated 3–6 months from application)
Execute deed of covenant between charity and trading subsidiary
Submit first AHF Project Development Grant application
